htmlContent:
The body doesn't forget

I remember the pressure. The weight of someone's knee on my chest. The feeling of my wrists being pinned above my head. The sound of my own breath getting trapped somewhere between my throat and my lungs. And I remember thinking, over and over, just stay still. Just stop moving. Maybe if I go limp, they'll let go.

That memory isn't just in my head. It's in my shoulders, which still tighten when someone touches me from behind. It's in my jaw, which clenches when I feel trapped in a crowded room. It's in my ribs, which ache when I hear footsteps approaching too quickly. The body stores the memory of being physically restrained. Not as a story, but as a sensation. A pattern. A reflex that fires before thought can catch up.

Here's the thing. When someone holds you down, your nervous system doesn't just register the event and move on. It encodes the experience into your tissues, your muscles, your fascia. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, spent decades studying how animals and humans process physical threat. He found that when we are restrained and cannot complete the natural survival response of fight or flight, the energy of that thwarted response gets trapped in the body. It stays there, waiting for a chance to complete itself. Waiting for a moment that never comes.

Think about it. If you've ever been held down, pinned, strapped in, or physically overpowered, your body learned something that day. It learned that struggling doesn't work. It learned that your boundaries can be violated regardless of what you want. It learned that the people around you might not stop when you say stop. And that learning isn't intellectual. It's cellular.

What happens to the nervous system during restraint

When you are physically restrained, your nervous system moves through a predictable sequence. First, there's the fight response. Your body floods with adrenaline, your heart rate spikes, your muscles tense. You push. You pull. You try to get free. But if the restraint continues and escape proves impossible, something shifts.

Your nervous system switches to a different survival strategy. It's called the dorsal vagal response, a term from Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory. This is the freeze state. Your body goes limp. Your heart rate drops. Your breathing becomes shallow. You might feel numb, dissociated, or strangely calm. This isn't acceptance. It's a biological shutdown designed to make you invisible to predators. To reduce the pain of what's happening. To conserve energy until the threat passes.

But here's what most people don't understand. The energy of the fight response doesn't just disappear when you freeze. It gets stored. The tension in your muscles, the pressure in your chest, the heat in your face, the impulse to push back, all of it gets locked into your nervous system like a paused video. And it stays paused until your body feels safe enough to complete the movement.

I worked with a client once who had been held down during a medical procedure as a child. Thirty years later, she couldn't lie flat on a massage table without panicking. Her body remembered the restraint even though her mind had mostly forgotten the details. She would feel her shoulders rise toward her ears, her legs would stiffen, her throat would close. That's the memory of restraint living in the body, not as a thought but as a physical reality.

Wild, right? The body doesn't care about time. It only cares about survival.

Where restraint lives in the body

The body stores the memory of being restrained in very specific places. The most common areas are the shoulders, the jaw, the hips, the chest, and the wrists. But honestly, it can show up anywhere depending on how you were held.

If someone pinned your arms, the tension might live in your shoulders and upper back. You might notice that you carry your shoulders hunched forward, as if bracing for impact. Or that your arms feel heavy and hard to lift. Or that you have chronic tension in your neck from the effort of keeping your head still.

If someone held your legs, the memory might settle in your hips and thighs. You might find yourself crossing your legs tightly, or feeling a sense of trapped energy in your pelvis. You might have trouble relaxing your legs during sleep, or feel a constant urge to stretch them out, to move, to escape a position that feels too fixed.

If someone pressed on your chest or throat, the memory might live in your breathing. Your ribs might feel tight. Your diaphragm might be locked in a shallow pattern. You might find yourself holding your breath without realizing it, or sighing frequently as your body tries to release the stored tension.

One of the most effective ways I've found to address this stored tension is through gentle, intentional movement. Pat Ogden, who developed Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, talks about how the body needs to complete the action that was interrupted during the trauma. If you were held down, your body might need to slowly, safely push against something. To extend your arms. To stretch your legs. To feel your own strength in a context where you are in control.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it on your back, hips, and shoulders can help your body feel the sensation of pressure in a way that you control. You choose how much weight to apply. You decide when to stop. That sense of agency is crucial for a nervous system that once learned it had no say.

The slow work of releasing the grip

Releasing the memory of restraint is not about trying to force your body to relax. That usually backfires. Telling yourself to just let go when your body has learned that letting go is dangerous can actually increase the tension. Your nervous system interprets that command as a threat. It tightens more.

The work is slower than that. It starts with noticing. You might notice that your shoulders are tight. Don't try to fix it yet. Just notice. Feel the quality of the tension. Is it hard and bony? Is it more like a dull ache? Is it hot or cold? Stay with the sensation for a few breaths without trying to change it.

Then, you can experiment with tiny movements. Maybe you roll your shoulders back an inch. Maybe you tilt your head to the side. Maybe you let your jaw drop open slightly. The key is to move slowly, with awareness, and to stop if you feel any increase in fear or panic. Your body needs to know that you are paying attention, that you are in charge, that you will stop if something feels wrong.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium plays a role in regulating the nervous system and relaxing muscles. For bodies that hold chronic tension from past restraint, gentle nutritional support can make the process of releasing a little less overwhelming.

I also find that working with the breath can be powerful, but you have to be careful. If your breathing was restricted during the restraint, focusing too much on the breath can trigger panic. A gentler approach is to simply notice your exhale. Let it be longer than your inhale if that feels safe. But don't force it. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It gives your breath a physical anchor, which can feel grounding when the memory of restraint starts to surface.

And sometimes, the most important thing is to reduce the sensory input that might trigger the memory. Loud noises, sudden movements, or unexpected touch can all activate the body's memory of being held down. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). Wearing them in overwhelming environments can help your nervous system stay regulated, giving your body a chance to feel safe in its own space.

You are not broken

I want to say something directly to you. If your body holds the memory of being physically restrained, you are not broken. You are not weak. You are not overreacting. Your body did exactly what it was designed to do. It survived. It stored the information it needed to protect you in the future. That's not a flaw. That's intelligence.

The problem is that the information is outdated. Your body is still bracing for a threat that happened in the past. It doesn't know that you are safe now, that you have choices now, that you can say no and mean it now. So the work is about teaching your body, gently and repeatedly, that the present moment is different from the past.

That takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to feel uncomfortable sensations without immediately trying to escape them. And it takes support. You don't have to do this alone. Working with a therapist who understands somatic approaches can make a huge difference. Someone who can help you track what your body is doing, who can help you stay present when the fear rises, who can remind you that you are here, now, and you are safe.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Maybe you felt a small release. Maybe you tightened up. Maybe you went numb. Whatever it was, it's okay. That's just your body doing its job. It's trying to protect you. And the more you can meet it with curiosity instead of judgment, the more it will learn that it can let go.

I still feel the memory of being restrained in my own body. Some days it's a whisper, a slight tension in my wrists when I'm typing. Some days it's a roar, a full-body panic that makes me want to crawl out of my skin. But I've learned to work with it instead of against it. I've learned to listen. I've learned to move slowly. And I've learned that my body is not my enemy. It's my oldest ally, trying to keep me safe with the only tools it has.

Stay with me here. The memory of restraint doesn't have to define your life. It can be something you carry without being crushed by it. It can be something you understand, something you work with, something that teaches you about your own resilience. Your body is not a prison. It's a living record of everything you've survived. And it's capable of healing, one tiny release at a time.
